costs: Transport infrastructure investment for grades 5-8 consolidation routes
Dual-track curriculum/staffing planning cost during transition
Retraining cost for multigrade pedagogy in retained lower-grade schools
content.equity_impact: May reduce the acute harms of long commutes for the youngest and most vulnerable children while concentrating upper-grade resources in larger schools; must be paired with non-discrimination safeguards given catchment-based decisions are subject to legal constraints following Szolcsán v. Hungary (F-live-portuguese_reform-09), and does not by itself resolve church-school segregation dynamics.
expected_benefits: Preserves community hub function and short commutes for youngest, most commute-vulnerable children
Allows upper-grade pupils access to broader curriculum, specialist teachers, and facilities unavailable in small village schools
Provides an intermediate policy option between full retention and full closure, potentially more fiscally sustainable long-term
content.goal: Preserve early-years village schooling (grades 1-4) while consolidating upper primary grades into larger, better-resourced town schools, following the structural logic of the Polish 1999 gimnazjum-style reorganization adapted to Hungarian primary-grade structure.

implementation_steps.action: Define national criteria and pilot regions for grade-split structural reorganization, selecting settlements with viable grades 1-4 enrollment but declining grades 5+ enrollment
Designate consolidated ISCED2-type upper-grade schools in town centers and establish transport routes for grades 5-8 pupils
Develop mandatory multigrade/mixed-age pedagogy training and cross-school professional learning networks for retained lower-grade village schools

mechanisms: Poland's 1999 gimnazjum reform functioned as a structural reorganization lever distinct from simple closure, consolidating pupil access across neighbouring villages while formally preserving some primary buildings (F-live-polish_reform-03).

Poland's 2017 reversal of this reform was explicitly political rather than evidence-based, disregarding positive PISA and labour-market evidence, and caused acute disruption including displacement of 10,000 teachers and unplaced student cohorts, indicating high transition risk if reorganization is later reversed abruptly (F-live-polish_reform-04, F-live-polish_reform-05).

Younger children (grades 1-4) benefit most from proximity given documented harms of long rural bus commutes (breakfast-skipping, lower grades, reduced social activity), while older students can more feasibly travel to a consolidated school (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10).

Multigrade/mixed-age teaching quality depends on deliberate teacher training and cross-school professional networks, as shown in non-Hungarian systems (Chile, Norway), which would be required to support the retained lower-grade village schools (F-live-portuguese_reform-07).
risks: Reversal risk if structural reform is later abandoned for political reasons, as in Poland 2017, with severe disruption to displaced teachers and students (F-live-polish_reform-04, F-live-polish_reform-05)
Older students' commute burden shifts rather than disappears, and current rural transport infrastructure is documented as impractical in several regions (F-live-school_network_planning-07)
Risk that even lower-grade retention remains fiscally exposed to national class-size minimums (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04)
content.title: Structural grade split: village-based lower grades with centrally sited upper-grade schools
